title: Reupholstering an ottoman lid with foam and fabric
subtitle: Strip, pad, and re-cover a flat ottoman lid with new foam and fabric
excerpt: How to remove old fabric and foam from an ottoman lid, cut and fit a new CMHR foam pad, wrap in Dacron for a rounded edge profile, cut the cover fabric on the grain, stretch and staple it to the underside of the lid, and finish with a dust cover.
sourceNotes: The Complete Guide to Upholstery, Cherry Dobson. Furniture and Fittings Industry Standards (FIRA). UK Furniture and Furnishings (Fire Safety) Regulations 1988.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combustion-modified high-resilience polyurethane foam — the standard foam required by UK law in all upholstered furniture manufactured or significantly repaired after 1988. The combustion modification reduces the rate at which the foam contributes to a fire. Available in densities from soft (28 kg/m³) to firm (45 kg/m³); an ottoman seat uses 35 to 45 kg/m³ firm grade.

A layer of polyester fibre wadding wrapped around the foam to soften the hard corners of the foam block and give the finished cushion a slightly rounded, upholstered appearance. One or two layers of 50 mm Dacron are standard for a flat ottoman lid.

Cutting upholstery fabric so that the warp threads (threads running along the length of the roll) run straight along the length of the piece being cut. Fabric cut off-grain will appear to pull diagonally once tensioned, causing stripe patterns to lean. Always check the grain before cutting by pulling a single warp thread from one cut edge to confirm the direction.

content.text: An ottoman lid is the simplest upholstery project: a flat timber panel, a layer of
CMHR foam
, a layer of
Dacron
, and a fabric cover stapled to the underside. The key skill is achieving even tension across the fabric. Uneven pull distorts a pattern or drags the fabric off-grain.
Step 1, strip the old cover and clean the board

Remove the old staples with the staple remover: work methodically along each side, using the forked tip to lift each staple before pulling with pliers. Remove all old fabric, foam, and Dacron. Inspect the timber lid for any loose areas or sharp staple remnants and remove or flatten anything that would show through the new fabric. Sand any rough spots on the underside edges where the fabric will wrap.
Step 2, cut and fit the foam
Measure the lid and cut the
CMHR foam

to exactly the same size as the lid using the utility knife with a long straight cut guided by a straightedge. The foam should sit exactly at the lid edge: not overhanging. Glue the foam to the lid with spray adhesive or contact adhesive on both surfaces; press and allow to tack.
Step 3, wrap with Dacron
Cut a piece of
Dacron

approximately 100 mm larger than the lid on all sides. Lay the Dacron over the foam face, smooth it from the centre outward, and fold it over the edges and around to the underside. Staple it to the underside of the board at the edge, pulling it just taut enough to keep the Dacron against the foam without compressing the top surface. Trim the Dacron corners so they do not bunch.
Step 4, cut and apply the fabric
Cut the fabric on the
grain

, 150 mm larger than the lid on all sides. Lay the fabric face down on a clean surface, place the padded lid face down on top, and centre it. Starting at the centre of each long side, pull the fabric to the underside and staple with 8 mm staples at 50 mm intervals, alternating between opposite sides. Always pull toward the opposite side before stapling the next side: this maintains even tension. Work from the centre of each side toward the corners, leaving the corners until last.
Step 5, fold the corners and fit a dust cover

At each corner, fold the fabric neatly: either a hospital fold (for a square corner) or a pleated fold (for a rounded corner): and staple firmly. Trim any excess fabric on the underside close to the staple line. Cut a piece of black dust cover fabric slightly smaller than the lid underside and staple it over the raw fabric edges, turning the edges under as you go.
Variations

For a tufted ottoman lid, a grid of button positions is marked on the fabric and foam before stapling. A long upholstery needle is used to pull button twine through the foam and board from above, and the button is tied off on the underside. Tufting compresses the foam in a diamond pattern and requires cutting the foam slightly oversize (the tufting draws it in).

attrs.symptom: Fabric pulls diagonally across the lid after stapling
attrs.cause: Fabric cut off-grain; uneven tension applied — more pull on one side than the other.
attrs.fix: Remove all staples. Realign the fabric so the warp runs straight along the lid length. Restaple from the centre of each side, keeping equal pull on opposite sides.

attrs.symptom: Bumpy ridges visible on the lid surface under the fabric
attrs.cause: Old staple remnants or rough timber not removed before repadding; Dacron bunching under the fabric.
attrs.fix: Remove the fabric and Dacron. Inspect the board surface and remove all staple remnants. Smooth the Dacron from the centre before re-covering.

attrs.symptom: Fabric corners look thick and lumpy
attrs.cause: Too much fabric gathered at the corner rather than folded neatly; excess Dacron at the corner.
attrs.fix: Trim the Dacron at the corners so there is only a single layer wrapping around each corner. On the fabric, use a single hospital fold: fold one side first, then fold the adjacent side over it cleanly.
